pages.title: Flat morphisms
pages.html:
IV Flat morphisms

We give here above all those properties of flatness which served us in the preceding exposés. A more detailed study will be found in Chapter IV of the “Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique” in preparation1, where one studies in a systematic way the following situation: \(X\) being locally of finite type over \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and \(\mathscr{F}\) coherent on \(X\) and \(Y\)-flat, to give relations between the properties of \(Y\), those of \(\mathscr{F}\), and those of the coherent sheaves induced by \(\mathscr{F}\) on the fibres of \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) (from the point of view notably of dimension, of cohomological dimension, of depth etc.). One has in particular a systematic way of obtaining theorems of the type Seidenberg or Bertini (for hyperplane sections). The essential result for the application of the methods of flatness in this context is the following (which will be proved below): If \(Y\) is integral, \(X\) of finite type over \(Y\), \(\mathscr{F}\) coherent on \(X\), there exists a non-empty open set \(U\) of \(Y\) such that \(\mathscr{F}\) is \(Y\)-flat at the points of \(X\) above \(U\). A second way, doubtless even more important, in which flatness is introduced in Algebraic Geometry, is the theory of descent: see for example the two exposés of Grothendieck on the subject at the Séminaire Bourbaki2. Flatness thus appears as one of the central technical notions in Algebraic Geometry.

Let us recall that the notion of flatness and faithful flatness was introduced by Serre in GAGA. An account of the following 1 and 2 will also be found in Alg. Comm. of Bourbaki (which of course, as the title of the book indicates, is not confined to the case of commutative base rings)3.

Contrary to the preceding exposés, we do not suppose that the rings under consideration are necessarily Noetherian.

pages.title: Sorites on flat modules
pages.html:
1 Sorites on flat modules

A module \(M\) over the ring \(A\) is called flat (or \(A\)-flat if one wishes to specify \(A\)) if the functor

\[ T_M \colon N \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} M \otimes_{A}N \]

(which is in any case right exact) is exact, i.e. transforms monomorphisms into monomorphisms. It amounts to the same to say that the first right derived functor, or all the right derived functors, are zero, i.e. that we have.5cm

\[ \operatorname{Tor}^{A}_{1}(M,N)=0 \qquad \text{for all $N$,} \]

resp. that we have

\[ \operatorname{Tor}^{A}_{i}(M,N)=0 \qquad \text{for $i>0$, all $N$.} \]

Since the \(\operatorname{Tor}_{i}\) commute with direct limits, it suffices moreover to verify these conditions for \(N\) of finite type, and even (then taking a composition series of \(N\) with monogenic quotients) that we have

\[ \operatorname{Tor}^{A}_{1}(M,N)=0 \]

if \(N\) is monogenic, i.e. of the form \(A/I\), \(I\) being an ideal of \(A\). Let us note moreover that

\[ \operatorname{Tor}^{A}_{1}(M,A/I)=0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad I\otimes_{A}M \rightarrow M=A\otimes_{A}M\text{ is \emph{injective}}, \]

as one sees from the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\), taking into account \(\operatorname{Tor}^{A}_{1}(M,A)=0\). Thus \(M\) flat is equivalent to saying that for every ideal \(I\), the natural homomorphism

\[ I\otimes_{A}M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} IM \]

is an isomorphism. It suffices moreover to verify this for \(I\) of finite type, a fortiori it suffices to verify that the functor \(M\otimes\) is exact on modules of finite type.

As whenever one has an exact functor \(T\), if one identifies, for a subobject \(N'\) of \(N\), \(T(N')\) with a subobject of \(T(N)\), one has

\[ T(N' \cap N'')=T(N') \cap T(N'') \]
\[ T(N' + N'')=T(N') + T(N'') \]

for two subobjects \(N'\), \(N''\) of \(N\).

A direct sum of flat modules, a direct factor of a flat module, is flat. In particular, \(A\) being flat, a free module, hence also a projective module, is flat. The tensor product of two flat modules is flat, and if \(M\) is flat over \(A\), then \(M\otimes_{A}B\) is flat over \(B\) for every base change \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) (because of the associativity of the tensor product and of the fact that a composite of exact functors is exact). If \(M\) is flat over \(B\), \(B\) flat over \(A\), then \(M\) is flat over \(A\) (same reason).

The exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\), plus the “commutativity” of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\), gives:

Proposition 1

Let \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) be an exact sequence of \(A\)-modules, \(M''\) being flat. Then

  1. (i)

    this sequence remains exact under tensorization by any \(A\)-module \(N\)

  2. (ii)

    for \(M\) to be flat, it is necessary and sufficient that \(M'\) be so.

One may thus say that from the point of view of behaviour under tensor products, flat modules are “as good as” free or projective modules (and the exact sequence of 1 in particular is “as good as” if it split).

Let \(S\) be a multiplicatively stable subset of \(A\); then \(S^{-1}A\) is flat over \(A\), since \(S^{-1}A \otimes N=S^{-1}N\) is a functor exact in \(N\). If \(M\) is \(A\)-flat, then \(S^{-1}M=S^{-1}A \otimes M\) is \(S^{-1}A\)-flat, the converse being true if \(M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{-1}M\) is an isomorphism, i.e. if the \(s \in S\) are bijective in \(M\) (because of the transitivity of flatness, \(S^{-1}A\) being flat over \(A\)). More generally, the case of a morphism of preschemes \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and of a quasi-coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X\) whose flatness relative to \(Y\) one wishes to study leads to the situation with two rings:

Proposition 2

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings, \(M\) a \(B\)-module, \(T\) a multiplicatively stable subset of \(B\).

  1. (i)

    If \(M\) is \(A\)-flat, then \(T^{-1}M\) is \(A\)-flat (hence also \(S^{-1}A\)-flat for every multiplicatively stable subset \(S\) of \(A\) mapping into \(T\)).

  2. (ii)

    Conversely, if \(M_{\mathfrak{n}}\) is flat over \(A_{\mathfrak{n}}\) for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}\) of \(B\), \(M_{\mathfrak{n}}\) is flat over \(A\) (or, what amounts to the same, over \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\), where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the prime ideal of \(A\) inverse image of \(\mathfrak{n}\)) then \(M\) is \(A\)-flat.

One has indeed the formula, functorial with respect to the \(A\)-module \(N\):

\[ T^{-1}M \otimes_{A} N=T^{-1}(M \otimes_{A} N) \]

for the two members are functorially isomorphic to \(T^{-1}B \otimes_{B} M \otimes_{B} N_{(B)}\) (with \(N_{(B)}=N \otimes_{A} B\)) by virtue of the associativity formulas of \(\otimes\). It follows immediately that if \(M \otimes_{A} N\) is exact in \(N\), so is \(T^{-1}M \otimes_{A} N\) (as a composite of two exact functors), whence (i). And (ii) follows likewise, for to verify the exactness of a sequence of \(B\)-modules, it suffices to verify the exactness of the localizations at all the maximal ideals of \(B\).

Proposition 3

  1. (i)

    Let \(M\) be a flat \(A\)-module. If \(x \in A\) is not a divisor of \(0\) in \(A\), it is not a divisor of \(0\) in \(M\). In particular, if \(A\) is integral, \(M\) is torsion-free.

  2. (ii)

    Suppose that \(A\) is integral and that for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\), \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) is principal (for example \(A\) a Dedekind ring, or even principal). For the \(A\)-module \(M\) to be flat, it is necessary and it suffices that it be torsion-free.

One obtains (i) by noting that the homothety \(x\) in \(M\) is obtained by tensoring by \(M\) the homothety \(x\) in \(A\). For (ii), one may already assume \(A\) principal thanks to 2 (ii); one must show that if \(M\) is torsion-free, then for every ideal \(I\) of \(A\), the injection \(I \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) tensored by \(M\) is an injection, which means that the generator \(x\) of \(I\) is not a divisor of \(0\) in \(M\), O.K.

pages.title: Faithfully flat modules
pages.html:
2 Faithfully flat modules

A functor \(F\) from one category to another is said to be faithful if for all \(X\), \(Y\), the map \(\operatorname{Hom} (X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom} (F(X),F(Y))\) is injective. If it is an additive functor of additive categories, it amounts to the same to say that \(F(u)=0\) implies \(u=0\), and this implies that \(F(X)=0\) implies \(X=0\). For \(F\) to be faithful and exact, it is necessary and sufficient that the following condition be satisfied: for every sequence \(M' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\) of morphisms in \(\mathscr{C}\), the transformed sequence \(F(M') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(M'')\) is exact if and only if the preceding one is. Or again: \(F\) is exact, and \(F(X)=0\) implies \(X=0\). (N.B. in order to be able to speak of exactness, one must suppose the categories in play abelian). Suppose that we have a family \((M_{i})\) of nonzero objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) such that every nonzero object of \(\mathscr{C}\) has a subobject admitting a quotient isomorphic to some \(M_{i}\). Then \(F\) faithful and exact is equivalent to: \(F\) exact, and \(F(M_{i}) \neq 0\) for all \(i\). If \(\mathscr{C}\) is the category of modules over a ring \(A\), one may take for example as \((M_{i})\) the family of the \(A/\mathfrak{m}\), \(\mathfrak{m}\) ranging over the maximal ideals of \(A\). (Indeed, every nonzero module admits a nonzero monogenic submodule, hence isomorphic to some \(A/I\), \(I\) an ideal \(\neq A\), which by Krull admits a quotient \(A/\mathfrak{m}\)). From these generalities, one deduces in particular:

Proposition 1

Let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    The functor \(M \otimes_{A}\) is faithful and exact.

  2. (i bis)

    \(M\) is flat, and \(M \otimes_{A} N=0\) implies \(N=0\)

  3. (i ter)

    \(M\) is flat, and \(M \otimes A/\mathfrak{m} \neq 0\) for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\).

  4. (ii)

    For every sequence of homomorphisms \(N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N''\), the sequence tensored by \(M\) is exact if and only if the initial sequence is.

One then says that \(M\) is a faithfully flat \(A\)-module. In particular, if \(M\) is faithfully flat, then \(N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N'\) is a monomorphism (epimorphism, isomorphism) if and only if the homomorphism tensored by \(M\) is. A faithfully flat module is faithful, since the homothety \(f\) in \(M\) is obtained by tensoring by \(M\) the homothety \(f\) in \(A\).

One sees as in 1 the usual transitivity properties: the tensor product of two faithfully flat modules is faithfully flat, if \(M\) is faithfully flat over \(A\), \(M \otimes_{A}B\) is faithfully flat over \(B\) for every extension of the base \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), if \(B\) is an \(A\)-algebra that is faithfully flat over \(A\) and if \(M\) is a faithfully flat \(B\)-module, it is a faithfully flat \(A\)-module.

Corollary 2

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings, \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type. For \(M\) to be faithfully flat over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be flat over \(A\) and nonzero.

Follows from criterion (i ter) and from Nakayama. In particular, for \(B\) to be \(A\)-flat, it is necessary and sufficient that it be faithfully \(A\)-flat.

Proposition 3

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings, \(M\) a \(B\)-module that is faithfully flat over \(A\). For every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\), there exists a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) that induces it.

Dividing by \(\mathfrak{p}\), one is reduced to the case where \(\mathfrak{p}=0\). Localizing at the prime ideal \(0\), one is reduced to the case where \(A\) is a field. But \(M\), being faithfully flat over \(A\), is nonzero, a fortiori \(B \neq 0\), so \(B\) has a prime ideal, which can only induce the unique prime ideal of \(A\)! Geometrically, one may say that the existence of a quasi-coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (B)\) that is “faithfully flat” relative to \(A\) implies that \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\) is surjective.

Corollary 4

Suppose \(M\) flat over \(A\), of finite type over \(B\) and \(\operatorname{Supp} M=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) (i.e. \(M_{\mathfrak{q}}\neq 0\) for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\)). Then the minimal prime ideals \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) containing \(\mathfrak{p}B\) induce \(\mathfrak{p}\).

One is again reduced to the case \(\mathfrak{p}=0\) (for the hypotheses are all preserved under dividing), hence \(A\) integral. One is reduced to the following statement:

Corollary 5

\(M\) being as above, every minimal prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) induces a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) that is minimal.

Indeed, localizing at \(\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\mathfrak{q}\), one is reduced to proving that if \(A\) and \(B\) are local and the homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) local, \(M\) a nonzero \(B\)-module flat over \(A\), and if \(B\) is of dimension \(0\), then \(A\) is of dimension \(0\). By 2 and 3, one concludes that every prime ideal of \(A\) is induced by a prime ideal of \(B\), hence by the maximal ideal of \(B\), hence is the maximal ideal, q.e.d. Geometrically, 5 means that every irreducible component of \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (B)\) dominates some irreducible component of \(Y=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\) (given the existence of a quasi-coherent sheaf of finite type on \(X\), of support \(X\), and flat relative to \(Y\)).

One will note that in 4 one did not have to suppose \(M\) faithfully flat over \(A\), but then nothing guarantees the existence of a prime ideal containing \(\mathfrak{p}B\) (hence of a minimal one among such).

Proposition 6

Let \(i \colon A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(B\) is a faithfully flat \(A\)-module.

  2. (ii)

    \(B\) is flat over \(A\), and \(\operatorname{Spec} (B) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} (A)\) is surjective

  3. (ii bis)

    \(B\) is flat over \(A\), and every maximal ideal is induced by an ideal of \(B\).

  4. (iii)

    \(i\) is injective and \(\operatorname{Coker} i\) is a flat \(A\)-module.

  5. (iv)

    The functor \(M_{(B)}=M \otimes_{A} B\) in the \(A\)-module \(M\) is exact, and the canonical functorial homomorphism \(M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M_{(B)}\) is injective.

  6. (iv bis)

    For every ideal \(I\) of \(A\), \(I \otimes_{A} B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} IB\) is an isomorphism, and the inverse image of \(IB\) in \(A\) is equal to \(I\).

We have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) by 3, (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis) is trivial, (ii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) by criterion (i ter) of 1. We have (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) by 1, (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv bis) trivially (by taking \(M=A/I\) in the second condition (iv bis)), and (iv bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) by virtue of the flatness criterion via ideals seen at the beginning of 1 and of criterion 1 (i ter). Finally, (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) by an easy converse of 1, and (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) because if \(N\) is the kernel of \(M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_{A}B =T(M)\), then (\(T\) being exact) \(N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(N)\) is zero whence \(T(N)=N \otimes_{A}B =0\), whence \(N=0\), q.e.d.

    The functor \(M \otimes_{A}\) is faithful and exact.

  2. (i bis)

    \(M\) is flat, and \(M \otimes_{A} N=0\) implies \(N=0\)

  3. (i ter)

    \(M\) is flat, and \(M \otimes A/\mathfrak{m} \neq 0\) for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\).

  4. (ii)

    For every sequence of homomorphisms \(N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N''\), the sequence tensored by \(M\) is exact if and only if the initial sequence is.

One then says that \(M\) is a faithfully flat \(A\)-module. In particular, if \(M\) is faithfully flat, then \(N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N'\) is a monomorphism (epimorphism, isomorphism) if and only if the homomorphism tensored by \(M\) is. A faithfully flat module is faithful, since the homothety \(f\) in \(M\) is obtained by tensoring by \(M\) the homothety \(f\) in \(A\).

One sees as in 1 the usual transitivity properties: the tensor product of two faithfully flat modules is faithfully flat, if \(M\) is faithfully flat over \(A\), \(M \otimes_{A}B\) is faithfully flat over \(B\) for every extension of the base \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), if \(B\) is an \(A\)-algebra that is faithfully flat over \(A\) and if \(M\) is a faithfully flat \(B\)-module, it is a faithfully flat \(A\)-module.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of local rings, \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type. For \(M\) to be faithfully flat over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be flat over \(A\) and nonzero.
Follows from criterion (i ter) and from Nakayama. In particular, for \(B\) to be \(A\)-flat, it is necessary and sufficient that it be faithfully \(A\)-flat.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings, \(M\) a \(B\)-module that is faithfully flat over \(A\). For every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\), there exists a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) that induces it.

Dividing by \(\mathfrak{p}\), one is reduced to the case where \(\mathfrak{p}=0\). Localizing at the prime ideal \(0\), one is reduced to the case where \(A\) is a field. But \(M\), being faithfully flat over \(A\), is nonzero, a fortiori \(B \neq 0\), so \(B\) has a prime ideal, which can only induce the unique prime ideal of \(A\)! Geometrically, one may say that the existence of a quasi-coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (B)\) that is “faithfully flat” relative to \(A\) implies that \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\) is surjective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Suppose \(M\) flat over \(A\), of finite type over \(B\) and \(\operatorname{Supp} M=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) (i.e. \(M_{\mathfrak{q}}\neq 0\) for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\)). Then the minimal prime ideals \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) containing \(\mathfrak{p}B\) induce \(\mathfrak{p}\).
One is again reduced to the case \(\mathfrak{p}=0\) (for the hypotheses are all preserved under dividing), hence \(A\) integral. One is reduced to the following statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

\(M\) being as above, every minimal prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) induces a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) that is minimal.

Indeed, localizing at \(\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\mathfrak{q}\), one is reduced to proving that if \(A\) and \(B\) are local and the homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) local, \(M\) a nonzero \(B\)-module flat over \(A\), and if \(B\) is of dimension \(0\), then \(A\) is of dimension \(0\). By 2 and 3, one concludes that every prime ideal of \(A\) is induced by a prime ideal of \(B\), hence by the maximal ideal of \(B\), hence is the maximal ideal, q.e.d. Geometrically, 5 means that every irreducible component of \(X=\operatorname{Spec} (B)\) dominates some irreducible component of \(Y=\operatorname{Spec} (A)\) (given the existence of a quasi-coherent sheaf of finite type on \(X\), of support \(X\), and flat relative to \(Y\)).

One will note that in 4 one did not have to suppose \(M\) faithfully flat over \(A\), but then nothing guarantees the existence of a prime ideal containing \(\mathfrak{p}B\) (hence of a minimal one among such).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6

Let \(i \colon A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(B\) is a faithfully flat \(A\)-module.

  2. (ii)

    \(B\) is flat over \(A\), and \(\operatorname{Spec} (B) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} (A)\) is surjective

  3. (ii bis)

    \(B\) is flat over \(A\), and every maximal ideal is induced by an ideal of \(B\).

  4. (iii)

    \(i\) is injective and \(\operatorname{Coker} i\) is a flat \(A\)-module.

  5. (iv)

    The functor \(M_{(B)}=M \otimes_{A} B\) in the \(A\)-module \(M\) is exact, and the canonical functorial homomorphism \(M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M_{(B)}\) is injective.

  6. (iv bis)

    For every ideal \(I\) of \(A\), \(I \otimes_{A} B \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} IB\) is an isomorphism, and the inverse image of \(IB\) in \(A\) is equal to \(I\).

We have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) by 3, (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis) is trivial, (ii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) by criterion (i ter) of 1. We have (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) by 1, (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv bis) trivially (by taking \(M=A/I\) in the second condition (iv bis)), and (iv bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) by virtue of the flatness criterion via ideals seen at the beginning of 1 and of criterion 1 (i ter). Finally, (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) by an easy converse of 1, and (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) because if \(N\) is the kernel of \(M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_{A}B =T(M)\), then (\(T\) being exact) \(N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(N)\) is zero whence \(T(N)=N \otimes_{A}B =0\), whence \(N=0\), q.e.d.

pages.title: Relations with completion
pages.html:
3 Relations with completion

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(I\) an ideal in \(A\), \(\widehat{A}\) the separated completion of \(A\) for the \(I\)-preadic topology, and for every \(A\)-module \(M\), let \(\widehat{M}\) be its completion for the \(I\)-preadic topology. It is an \(\widehat{A}\)-module, whence a canonical homomorphism

\[ M \otimes_A \widehat{A} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{M}. \]

When \(M\) ranges over the modules of finite type, the functor \(M \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \widehat{M}\) is exact, as follows easily from the Krull theorem: If \(N \subset M\), the topology of \(N\) is that induced by the topology of \(M\). Since \(M \otimes_A \widehat{A}\) is right exact, one concludes easily (by resolving \(M\) by \(L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\), with \(L\) and \(L'\) free of finite type) that the functorial homomorphism above is an isomorphism (\(\widehat{M}\) being also right exact) and consequently that \(M \otimes_A \widehat{A}\) is also a functor exact in \(M\). Accordingly:

Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\); then the separated completion \(\widehat{A}\) of \(A\) (for the \(I\)-preadic topology) is flat over \(A\).

Corollary 2

For \(\widehat{A}\) to be faithfully flat over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(I\) be contained in the radical of \(A\).

Indeed, it suffices to apply criterion 1 (i ter).

These results sum up everything one can say, from the point of view of linear algebra, about the relations between \(A\) and \(\widehat{A}\). Corollary 2 is used above all when \(A\) is a Noetherian local ring and \(I\) is contained in the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) (and most often is equal to it).

pages.title: Relations with free modules
pages.html:
4 Relations with free modules

Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Suppose that we are under one or the other of the following hypotheses:

  1. (a)

    \(I\) is nilpotent

  2. (b)

    \(A\) is Noetherian, \(I\) is in the radical of \(A\), and \(M\) is of finite type.

For \(M\) to be free over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(M \otimes A/I\) be free over \(A/I\), and that \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I) = 0\).

This is necessary; let us prove sufficiency. Let \((e_i)\) be a family of elements of \(M\) whose image in \(M \otimes A/I = M/IM\) there defines a basis over \(A/I\) (this is a finite family in case (b)). Let \(L\) be the free \(A\)-module constructed on the same set of indices; we thus have a homomorphism \(L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\) such that the tensorization \(T\) by \(A/I\) induces an isomorphism \(T(L) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} T(M)\). If \(Q\) is the cokernel of \(L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\), we thus have \(T(Q) = 0\), whence \(Q=0\) by virtue of Nakayama (valid under one or the other condition (a) or (b)). Hence \(L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\) is surjective; let \(R\) be its kernel, so we have an exact sequence

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

whence, since \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I)=0\), an exact sequence \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(R) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(L) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T(M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), whence \(T(R)=0\), whence again \(R=0\) by virtue of Nakayama (taking into account that in case (b), \(R\) is of finite type since \(A\) was assumed Noetherian).

Corollary 2

One may replace the condition \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I)=0\) by: the canonical surjective homomorphism

\[ \tag{*} { \operatorname{gr}_I^0(M) \otimes_{A/I} \operatorname{gr}_I(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}_I(M) } \]

is an isomorphism.

Indeed, if \(M\) is free, this is certainly satisfied. It therefore remains to prove that if \(M \otimes A/I\) is free over \(A/I\) and the condition on the \(\operatorname{gr}\) is satisfied, then \(M\) is free. One resumes the proof above by constructing \(L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\); it follows from the hypothesis that this homomorphism induces an isomorphism for the associated graded objects, hence its kernel is contained in the intersection of the \(I^nL\), hence is zero (as it is trivial in (a), and well known in (b)). Q.e.d.

Corollary 3

Suppose that \(A/I\) is a field. Then the following conditions on \(M\) are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is free

  2. (ii)

    \(M\) is projective

  3. (iii)

    \(M\) is flat

  4. (iv)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M, A/I) = 0\)

  5. (v)

    The canonical homomorphism (*) is bijective.

Indeed, in the case envisaged, \(M \otimes A/I\) is automatically free.

The preceding result is valid in the two following cases:

  1. (a)

    \(M\) is an arbitrary module over a local ring \(A\) whose maximal ideal \(I\) is nilpotent (for example an Artinian local ring).

  2. (b)

    \(M\) is a module of finite type over a local Noetherian ring.

Let us recall for the record:

Corollary 4

Suppose that \(A\) is an integral local Noetherian ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m} = I\), residue field \(k = A/I\), field of fractions \(K\). Let \(M\) be a module of finite type over \(A\). Then the equivalent conditions (i) to (v) above are also equivalent to .5cm

  1. (vi)

    \(M \otimes_A K\) and \(M \otimes_A k\) are vector spaces of the same dimension (i.e. the rank of \(M\) over \(A\) is equal to the minimum number of generators of the \(A\)-module \(M\)).

Immediate proof; we leave to the reader the task of generalizing to the case where \(A\) is only assumed to be without nilpotent elements: one must then require that the ranks of \(M\) for the minimal prime ideals of \(A\) be equal to the dimension of the vector space \(M \otimes_A k\).

pages.title: Local criteria of flatness
pages.html:
5 Local criteria of flatness

Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a ring equipped with an ideal \(I\), and \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Suppose

\[ \operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M, A/I^n) = 0 \qquad \text{for $n>0$} \]

then the canonical surjective homomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{gr}_I^0(M) \otimes_{A/I} \operatorname{gr}_I(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}_I(M)} \]

is an isomorphism. The converse is true if \(I\) is nilpotent.

The hypothesis means that the homomorphisms

\[ I^n \otimes_A M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I^n M \]

are isomorphisms, whence at once the fact that the homomorphisms

\[ I^n / I^{n+1} \otimes_A M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I^n M / I^{n+1} M \]

are isomorphisms. Conversely, suppose this condition satisfied and \(I\) nilpotent, let us prove \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I^n) =0\) for every \(n\). It is true for \(n\) large; let us proceed by descending induction on \(n\), supposing it proved for \(n+1\). We have a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ &{M \otimes I^{n+1}}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{M \otimes I^n}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{M \otimes(I^n/I^{n+1})}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{0}\\{0}\ar[r]&{MI^{n+1}}\ar[r]&{MI^n}\ar[r]&{MI^n/MI^{n+1}}\ar[r]&{0}} \]

where the rows are exact. By hypothesis, the last vertical arrow is an isomorphism, and the induction hypothesis also means that the first vertical arrow is one. The same is therefore true of the middle vertical arrow, which completes the proof.

The following proposition was brought to light at the time of the Seminar by Serre; it allows substantial simplifications in the present section.

Proposition 2

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings, and \(M\) an \(A\)-module. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    For every \(B\)-module \(N\), we have \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N) = 0\);

  2. (ii)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,B) = 0\), and \(M_{(B)} = M \otimes_A B\) is \(B\)-flat.

We have a functorial isomorphism

\[ M \otimes_A N = (M \otimes_A B) \otimes_B N \]

which exhibits the first member, considered as a functor in \(M\), as a composite of two functors \(M \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} M \otimes_A B\) and \(P \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} P \otimes_B N\). Since the first transforms free modules over \(A\) into free modules over \(B\), hence projectives into projectives, we have the spectral sequence of composite functors

\[ \operatorname{Tor}_n^A(M,N) \mathchoice{\Longleftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_p^B(\operatorname{Tor}_q^A(M,B),N) \]

whence an exact sequence for the terms of low degree

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_1^B(M \otimes_A B,N) \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N) \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,B) \otimes_A N \]

If (i) is satisfied, then from this exact sequence we conclude \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^B(M \otimes_A B,N) = 0\) for every \(N\), i.e. \(M \otimes_A B\) is \(B\)-flat, whence (ii). If conversely (ii) is satisfied, then in the exact sequence the terms surrounding \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N)\) are zero, hence we have (i).

Corollary 3

Suppose that \(B = A/I\); then the preceding conditions are equivalent to the following:

  1. (iii)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N) = 0\) for every \(A\)-module \(N\) annihilated by a power of \(I\).

Indeed, (i) means that this is so if \(N\) is annihilated by \(I\). One deduces (iii) from it by applying the hypothesis to the \(I^nN/I^{n+1}N\).

Corollary 4

Under the conditions of 3, the conditions envisaged imply that the functorial homomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{gr}_I^0(M) \otimes_{A/I} \operatorname{gr}_I(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}_I(M)} \]

is an isomorphism, and that \(M \otimes_A A/I\) is flat over \(A/I\).

It suffices to apply (iii) and (1). Using the converse of 1 in the case where \(I\) is nilpotent, one finds:

Corollary 5

Let \(A\) be a ring equipped with a nilpotent ideal \(I\), and \(M\) an \(A\)-module. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is \(A\)-flat

  2. (ii)

    \(M \otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I) = 0\)

  3. (iii)

    \(M \otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and the canonical homomorphism (*) on the associated graded objects is an isomorphism.

Indeed, these are respectively the preceding conditions (iii) and (ii), and those of Corollary 4.

Let us no longer suppose \(I\) nilpotent; then a priori we shall have only in 5 the implications (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii). On the other hand, since condition (iii) remains stable under division by a power of \(I\), one sees, by virtue of 5, that it implies

  1. (iv)

    for every integer \(n\), \(M \otimes A/I^n\) is flat over \(A/I^n\).

We propose to give conditions under which one can conclude (i) from this, i.e. that \(M\) is \(A\)-flat. I claim that for this it suffices that \(A\) be Noetherian and that \(M\) satisfy the following finiteness condition: for every module of finite type \(N\) over \(A\), \(M \otimes_A N\) is separated for the \(I\)-preadic topology. (It would suffice to verify this if \(N\) is an ideal of finite type in \(A\)). Indeed, let us prove that under these conditions, if \(N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N\) is a monomorphism of modules of finite type, \(M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N\) is a monomorphism. Indeed, it suffices to show that the kernel is contained in the \(I^n(M \otimes_A N') = \operatorname{Im}(M \otimes_A I^n N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N')\), or again in the \(\operatorname{Im}(M \otimes_A V'_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N') = \operatorname{Ker} (M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A (N'/V'_n))\), where \(V'_n\) runs through a countable fundamental system of neighbourhoods of 0 in \(N'\) (equipped with its \(I\)-adic topology). By Krull's theorem, the \(I\)-adic topology of \(N'\) is induced by that of \(N\), so one may take \(V'_n = N' \cap I^n N\). Let us then consider the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{M \otimes_A N'}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{M \otimes_A (N'/V'_n)}\ar[d]\\{M \otimes_A N}\ar[r]&{M \otimes_A (N / I^n N)}} \]

Since \(N'/V'_n\) and \(N/I^n N\) are annihilated by \(I^n\), the second vertical homomorphism is identified with the one deduced from the injective homomorphism \(N'/V'_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N/I^n N\) by tensoring over \(A/I^n\) with the flat \((A/I^n)\)-module \(M \otimes_A A/I^n\); it is therefore injective. Consequently, the kernel of \(M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N\) is contained in that of \(M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A (N'/V'_n),\) which is what we wanted.

The “finiteness” condition envisaged on \(M\) is satisfied in particular if \(M\) is a module of finite type over a Noetherian \(A\)-algebra \(B\) such that \(IB\) is contained in the radical of \(B\): indeed, then \(M \otimes_A N\) is a module of finite type over \(B\) for every module of finite type \(N\) over \(A\), hence separated by Krull for the \(I\)-adic topology \(=\) its \((IB)\)-adic topology. One thus finds:

Theorem 6

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of Noetherian rings, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\) such that \(IB\) is contained in the radical of \(B\), \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is \(A\)-flat

  2. (ii)

    \(M\otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I)=0\)

  3. (iii)

    \(M\otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and the canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{gr}_I^0(M)\otimes_{A/I}\operatorname{gr}_I(A)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{gr}_I(M) \]

    is an isomorphism.

  4. (iv)

    For every integer \(n\), \(M\otimes_A A/I^n\) is flat over \(A/I^n\).

This result applies above all when \(A\), \(B\) are Noetherian local rings, \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) a local homomorphism, and \(I\) an ideal of \(A\) contained in its maximal ideal (and one may immediately reduce 6 to this case). An interesting case is that where \(A/I\) is a field, i.e. \(I\) maximal, in which case the condition that \(M\otimes_A(A/I)\) be flat over \(A/I\) becomes superfluous; moreover, since then the \(A/I^n\) are Artinian local rings, condition (iv) means that the \(M\otimes_A(A/I^n)\) are free over the \(A/I^n\).

Corollary 7

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of Noetherian local rings, \(u\colon M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\) a homomorphism of \(B\)-modules of finite type, suppose \(M\) flat over \(A\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(u\) is injective, and \(\operatorname{Coker} u\) is flat over \(A\).

  2. (ii)

    \(u\otimes_A k\colon M'\otimes_A k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\otimes_A k\) is injective

(where \(k\) denotes the residue field of \(A\)).

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) by virtue of 1, let us prove the converse. First of all \(u\) is injective, for it suffices to verify this on the associated graded modules, where it follows from a commutative square that the reader will write down. Let \(M''\) be its \(\operatorname{Coker}\); we thus have an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

whence, by the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\), taking into account hypothesis (ii) and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,k)=0\), the relation \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M'',k)=0\), so that \(M''\) is flat over \(A\) by theorem 6.

Corollary 8

Under the conditions of 6, let \(J\) be an ideal of \(B\) containing \(IB\) and contained in the radical. Let \(\widehat{A}\) be the \(I\)-adic completion of \(A\), and \(\widehat{B}\) and \(\widehat{M}\) the \(J\)-adic completions of \(B\) and \(M\). For \(M\) to be \(A\)-flat, it is necessary and sufficient that \(M\) be \(\widehat{A}\)-flat.

(N.B. the sufficiency would already follow easily from 2). One uses criterion (iii) of 6 in the situation \((A,B,I,M)\) and in the situation \((\widehat{A},\widehat{B},I\widehat{A}, \widehat{M})\). One observes that the conditions obtained for the one case and the other are equivalent, thanks to 2.

Corollary 9

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) be local homomorphisms of Noetherian local rings, \(M\) a \(C\)-module of finite type (N.B. \(C\) intervenes only in order to be able to impose a finiteness condition on \(M\)). Suppose \(B\) flat over \(A\). Let \(k\) be the residue field of \(A\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is flat over \(B\).

  2. (ii)

    \(M\) is flat over \(A\), and \(M\otimes_A k\) is flat over \(B\otimes_A k\).

The implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is trivial, let us prove (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). One applies criterion (iii) of 6 to \((B,C,\mathfrak{m}B=I,M)\); since \(M\otimes_B(B/I)=M\otimes_B(B\otimes_A k) = M\otimes_A k\), the first condition of this criterion means precisely that \(M\otimes_A k\) is flat over \(B\otimes_A k\), perfect. The second condition of the criterion is satisfied because \(M\) is flat over \(A\) and \(B\) flat over \(A\), by an associativity formula for the tensor product. — Of course, referring to 5 instead of 6, one obtains an analogous statement without the Noetherian and finiteness condition, when one assumes instead that the ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\) is nilpotent. (The fact that \(\mathfrak{m}\) was taken maximal did not, moreover, intervene; but it is in a sense the case “\(\mathfrak{m}\) maximal” that is “the best possible”).

blocks.html: 5 Local criteria of flatness

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a ring equipped with an ideal \(I\), and \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Suppose

\[ \operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M, A/I^n) = 0 \qquad \text{for $n>0$} \]

then the canonical surjective homomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{gr}_I^0(M) \otimes_{A/I} \operatorname{gr}_I(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}_I(M)} \]

is an isomorphism. The converse is true if \(I\) is nilpotent.
The hypothesis means that the homomorphisms
\[ I^n \otimes_A M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I^n M \]
are isomorphisms, whence at once the fact that the homomorphisms
\[ I^n / I^{n+1} \otimes_A M \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I^n M / I^{n+1} M \]

are isomorphisms. Conversely, suppose this condition satisfied and \(I\) nilpotent, let us prove \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I^n) =0\) for every \(n\). It is true for \(n\) large; let us proceed by descending induction on \(n\), supposing it proved for \(n+1\). We have a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ &{M \otimes I^{n+1}}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{M \otimes I^n}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{M \otimes(I^n/I^{n+1})}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{0}\\{0}\ar[r]&{MI^{n+1}}\ar[r]&{MI^n}\ar[r]&{MI^n/MI^{n+1}}\ar[r]&{0}} \]

where the rows are exact. By hypothesis, the last vertical arrow is an isomorphism, and the induction hypothesis also means that the first vertical arrow is one. The same is therefore true of the middle vertical arrow, which completes the proof.
The following proposition was brought to light at the time of the Seminar by Serre; it allows substantial simplifications in the present section.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of rings, and \(M\) an \(A\)-module. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    For every \(B\)-module \(N\), we have \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N) = 0\);

  2. (ii)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,B) = 0\), and \(M_{(B)} = M \otimes_A B\) is \(B\)-flat.
We have a functorial isomorphism
\[ M \otimes_A N = (M \otimes_A B) \otimes_B N \]

which exhibits the first member, considered as a functor in \(M\), as a composite of two functors \(M \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} M \otimes_A B\) and \(P \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} P \otimes_B N\). Since the first transforms free modules over \(A\) into free modules over \(B\), hence projectives into projectives, we have the spectral sequence of composite functors
\[ \operatorname{Tor}_n^A(M,N) \mathchoice{\Longleftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow}{\Leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_p^B(\operatorname{Tor}_q^A(M,B),N) \]
whence an exact sequence for the terms of low degree

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_1^B(M \otimes_A B,N) \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N) \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,B) \otimes_A N \]

If (i) is satisfied, then from this exact sequence we conclude \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^B(M \otimes_A B,N) = 0\) for every \(N\), i.e. \(M \otimes_A B\) is \(B\)-flat, whence (ii). If conversely (ii) is satisfied, then in the exact sequence the terms surrounding \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N)\) are zero, hence we have (i).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Suppose that \(B = A/I\); then the preceding conditions are equivalent to the following:

  1. (iii)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,N) = 0\) for every \(A\)-module \(N\) annihilated by a power of \(I\).
Indeed, (i) means that this is so if \(N\) is annihilated by \(I\). One deduces (iii) from it by applying the hypothesis to the \(I^nN/I^{n+1}N\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Under the conditions of 3, the conditions envisaged imply that the functorial homomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{gr}_I^0(M) \otimes_{A/I} \operatorname{gr}_I(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{gr}_I(M)} \]

is an isomorphism, and that \(M \otimes_A A/I\) is flat over \(A/I\).
It suffices to apply (iii) and (1). Using the converse of 1 in the case where \(I\) is nilpotent, one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(A\) be a ring equipped with a nilpotent ideal \(I\), and \(M\) an \(A\)-module. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is \(A\)-flat

  2. (ii)

    \(M \otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I) = 0\)

  3. (iii)

    \(M \otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and the canonical homomorphism (*) on the associated graded objects is an isomorphism.
Indeed, these are respectively the preceding conditions (iii) and (ii), and those of Corollary 4.

Let us no longer suppose \(I\) nilpotent; then a priori we shall have only in 5 the implications (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii). On the other hand, since condition (iii) remains stable under division by a power of \(I\), one sees, by virtue of 5, that it implies

1. (iv)

    for every integer \(n\), \(M \otimes A/I^n\) is flat over \(A/I^n\).

We propose to give conditions under which one can conclude (i) from this, i.e. that \(M\) is \(A\)-flat. I claim that for this it suffices that \(A\) be Noetherian and that \(M\) satisfy the following finiteness condition: for every module of finite type \(N\) over \(A\), \(M \otimes_A N\) is separated for the \(I\)-preadic topology. (It would suffice to verify this if \(N\) is an ideal of finite type in \(A\)). Indeed, let us prove that under these conditions, if \(N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N\) is a monomorphism of modules of finite type, \(M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N\) is a monomorphism. Indeed, it suffices to show that the kernel is contained in the \(I^n(M \otimes_A N') = \operatorname{Im}(M \otimes_A I^n N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N')\), or again in the \(\operatorname{Im}(M \otimes_A V'_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N') = \operatorname{Ker} (M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A (N'/V'_n))\), where \(V'_n\) runs through a countable fundamental system of neighbourhoods of 0 in \(N'\) (equipped with its \(I\)-adic topology). By Krull's theorem, the \(I\)-adic topology of \(N'\) is induced by that of \(N\), so one may take \(V'_n = N' \cap I^n N\). Let us then consider the commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{M \otimes_A N'}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{M \otimes_A (N'/V'_n)}\ar[d]\\{M \otimes_A N}\ar[r]&{M \otimes_A (N / I^n N)}} \]

Since \(N'/V'_n\) and \(N/I^n N\) are annihilated by \(I^n\), the second vertical homomorphism is identified with the one deduced from the injective homomorphism \(N'/V'_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N/I^n N\) by tensoring over \(A/I^n\) with the flat \((A/I^n)\)-module \(M \otimes_A A/I^n\); it is therefore injective. Consequently, the kernel of \(M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A N\) is contained in that of \(M \otimes_A N' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M \otimes_A (N'/V'_n),\) which is what we wanted.

The “finiteness” condition envisaged on \(M\) is satisfied in particular if \(M\) is a module of finite type over a Noetherian \(A\)-algebra \(B\) such that \(IB\) is contained in the radical of \(B\): indeed, then \(M \otimes_A N\) is a module of finite type over \(B\) for every module of finite type \(N\) over \(A\), hence separated by Krull for the \(I\)-adic topology \(=\) its \((IB)\)-adic topology. One thus finds:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a homomorphism of Noetherian rings, \(I\) an ideal of \(A\) such that \(IB\) is contained in the radical of \(B\), \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is \(A\)-flat

  2. (ii)

    \(M\otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/I)=0\)

  3. (iii)

    \(M\otimes_A A/I\) is \(A/I\)-flat, and the canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{gr}_I^0(M)\otimes_{A/I}\operatorname{gr}_I(A)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{gr}_I(M) \]

    is an isomorphism.

  4. (iv)

    For every integer \(n\), \(M\otimes_A A/I^n\) is flat over \(A/I^n\).

This result applies above all when \(A\), \(B\) are Noetherian local rings, \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) a local homomorphism, and \(I\) an ideal of \(A\) contained in its maximal ideal (and one may immediately reduce 6 to this case). An interesting case is that where \(A/I\) is a field, i.e. \(I\) maximal, in which case the condition that \(M\otimes_A(A/I)\) be flat over \(A/I\) becomes superfluous; moreover, since then the \(A/I^n\) are Artinian local rings, condition (iv) means that the \(M\otimes_A(A/I^n)\) are free over the \(A/I^n\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of Noetherian local rings, \(u\colon M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\) a homomorphism of \(B\)-modules of finite type, suppose \(M\) flat over \(A\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(u\) is injective, and \(\operatorname{Coker} u\) is flat over \(A\).

  2. (ii)

    \(u\otimes_A k\colon M'\otimes_A k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\otimes_A k\) is injective

(where \(k\) denotes the residue field of \(A\)).

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) by virtue of 1, let us prove the converse. First of all \(u\) is injective, for it suffices to verify this on the associated graded modules, where it follows from a commutative square that the reader will write down. Let \(M''\) be its \(\operatorname{Coker}\); we thus have an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

whence, by the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\), taking into account hypothesis (ii) and \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,k)=0\), the relation \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M'',k)=0\), so that \(M''\) is flat over \(A\) by theorem 6.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Under the conditions of 6, let \(J\) be an ideal of \(B\) containing \(IB\) and contained in the radical. Let \(\widehat{A}\) be the \(I\)-adic completion of \(A\), and \(\widehat{B}\) and \(\widehat{M}\) the \(J\)-adic completions of \(B\) and \(M\). For \(M\) to be \(A\)-flat, it is necessary and sufficient that \(M\) be \(\widehat{A}\)-flat.

(N.B. the sufficiency would already follow easily from 2). One uses criterion (iii) of 6 in the situation \((A,B,I,M)\) and in the situation \((\widehat{A},\widehat{B},I\widehat{A}, \widehat{M})\). One observes that the conditions obtained for the one case and the other are equivalent, thanks to 2.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C\) be local homomorphisms of Noetherian local rings, \(M\) a \(C\)-module of finite type (N.B. \(C\) intervenes only in order to be able to impose a finiteness condition on \(M\)). Suppose \(B\) flat over \(A\). Let \(k\) be the residue field of \(A\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(M\) is flat over \(B\).

  2. (ii)

    \(M\) is flat over \(A\), and \(M\otimes_A k\) is flat over \(B\otimes_A k\).

The implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is trivial, let us prove (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). One applies criterion (iii) of 6 to \((B,C,\mathfrak{m}B=I,M)\); since \(M\otimes_B(B/I)=M\otimes_B(B\otimes_A k) = M\otimes_A k\), the first condition of this criterion means precisely that \(M\otimes_A k\) is flat over \(B\otimes_A k\), perfect. The second condition of the criterion is satisfied because \(M\) is flat over \(A\) and \(B\) flat over \(A\), by an associativity formula for the tensor product. — Of course, referring to 5 instead of 6, one obtains an analogous statement without the Noetherian and finiteness condition, when one assumes instead that the ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\) is nilpotent. (The fact that \(\mathfrak{m}\) was taken maximal did not, moreover, intervene; but it is in a sense the case “\(\mathfrak{m}\) maximal” that is “the best possible”).

pages.title: Flat morphisms and open sets
pages.html:
6 Flat morphisms and open sets

Let us first recall some results on constructible sets, which are moreover proved in notes in circulation from the Dieudonné-Rosenlicht Seminar on Schemes1.

Let \(X\) be a topological space. Following Chevalley, one says that a subset of \(X\) is constructible if it is a finite union of locally closed subsets.

Lemma 1

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space, let \(Z\) be a subset of \(X\). For \(Z\) to be constructible, it is necessary and sufficient that for every irreducible closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\), \(Z\cap Y\) be non-dense in \(Y\) or contain a non-empty open subset of the space \(Y\).

From this one deduces, using a well-known lemma of Commutative Algebra:

Lemma 2 (Chevalley)

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type of preschemes, with \(Y\) Noetherian. Then \(f(X)\) is constructible.

Lemma 3

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space every irreducible closed subset of which admits a generic point, \(U\) a constructible subset of \(X\), \(x\in X\). For \(U\) to be a neighbourhood of \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that every generization \(y\) of \(x\) (i.e. every \(y\in X\) such that \(x\in \overline{y}\)) lie in \(U\).

In particular

Corollary 4

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space every irreducible closed subset of which admits a generic point, \(U\) a subset of \(X\). For \(U\) to be open, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfy the two following conditions:

  1. (a)

    \(U\) contains every generization of each of its points

  2. (b)

    if \(x\in U\), then \(U\cap\overline{x}\) contains a non-empty open subset of the space \(\overline{x}\).

Indeed, \(U\) is necessarily constructible thanks to 1, and one applies the criterion 2 which proves that \(U\) is a neighbourhood of each of its points.

Corollary 5

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type of preschemes, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(y=f(x)\). For \(f\) to transform every neighbourhood of \(x\) into a neighbourhood of \(y\), it is necessary and sufficient that for every generization \(y'\) of \(y\), there exist a generization \(x'\) of \(x\) such that \(f(x')=y'\).

One may evidently assume that \(X\) and \(Y\) are affine, hence Noetherian. The condition is sufficient, for it suffices to prove that \(f(X)\) is a neighbourhood of \(y\); now \(f(X)\) is constructible by 1, and it suffices to apply the criterion 3. The condition is necessary, for let \(Y'=\overline{y'}\), and let \(F\) be the union of the irreducible components of \(f^{-1}(Y')\) which do not contain \(x\). Then \(X-F\) is an open neighbourhood of \(x\), hence its image is a neighbourhood of \(y\), and a fortiori contains \(y'\), so there exists \(x'_1\in X-F\) such that \(f(x'_1)=y'\). Consider an irreducible component of \(f^{-1}(Y')\) containing \(x'_1\); it necessarily contains \(x\) (for otherwise it would be contained in \(F\)); let \(x'\) be its generic point. It is a generization of \(x\), and \(f(x')\) is a generization of \(f(x'_1)=y'\) contained in \(Y'\), hence is equal to \(y'\), q.e.d.

Theorem 6

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(F\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\) of support \(X\), flat relative to \(Y\). Then \(f\) is an open morphism (i.e., transforms open sets into open sets).

It suffices to prove the criterion 5 for every point \(x\in X\). Now the generizations \(x'\) of \(x\) correspond to the prime ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), those \(y'\) of \(y\) correspond to the prime ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_y\), and one must therefore verify that every prime ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is induced by a prime ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_x\). Now \(F_x\) is a non-zero \(\mathscr{O}_x\)-module and \(\mathscr{O}_y\)-flat, hence faithfully flat over \(\mathscr{O}_y\) by 2. One may therefore apply 3, which completes the proof.

Remarks

Since flatness is preserved under base extension, one sees that under the conditions of 5 \(f\) is even universally open. I do not know, however, when \(Y\) is integral and \(X\) of finite type over \(Y\), whether \(f\) induces on every component \(X_i\) of \(X\) an open morphism, or even merely an equidimensional one2, i.e. one all of whose fibre components have the same dimension (one knows only that \(X_i\) dominates \(Y\)). The question is related to the following one: let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of Noetherian local rings, such that \(B\) is flat over \(A\) and \(\mathfrak{m}B\) is an ideal of definition of \(B\) (which moreover implies \(\dim B=\dim A\)). Is it true that for every minimal prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) of \(B\), one has \(\dim B/\mathfrak{p}_i=\dim B\)? Let us point out only that the answer to the first question is negative when one replaces the flatness hypothesis of 5 by the sole hypothesis that \(f\) be universally open.

Lemma 7

Let \(A\) be an integral Noetherian ring, \(B\) an \(A\)-algebra of finite type, \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type. Then there exists a non-zero element \(f\) of \(A\) such that \(M_f\) is a free module (a fortiori flat) over \(A_f\).

Let \(K\) be the field of fractions of \(A\); then \(B\otimes_A K\) is an algebra of finite type over \(K\), and \(M\otimes_A K\) a module of finite type over the latter. Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support of this module; we shall argue by induction on \(n\). If \(n<0\), i.e. if \(M\otimes_A K=0\), then taking a finite number of generators of \(M\) over \(B\), one sees that there exists an \(f\in A\) which annihilates these generators, hence \(M\), whence \(M_f=0\) and we have won. Suppose \(n\geqslant 0\). One knows that the \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a composition series whose successive quotients are isomorphic to modules \(B/\mathfrak{p}_i\), the \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) being prime ideals of \(B\). Since an extension of free modules is free, one is reduced to the case where \(M\) itself is of the form \(B/\mathfrak{p}\), or else identical to \(B\), \(B\) being an integral \(A\)-algebra. Applying Noether's normalization lemma to the \(K\)-algebra \(B\otimes_A K\), one sees easily that there exists a non-zero element \(f\) of \(A\) such that \(B_f\) is integral over the subring \(A_f[t_1,\dots,t_n]\), where the \(t_i\) are indeterminates. So one may already assume \(B\) is integral over \(C=A[t_1,\dots,t_n]\); it is therefore a \(C\)-module of finite type without torsion. Let \(m\) be its rank; there exists therefore an exact sequence of \(C\)-modules:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C^m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

where \(M'\) is a torsion \(C\)-module. It follows that the Krull dimension of the \(C\otimes_A K\)-module \(M'\otimes_A K\) is strictly less than that \(n\) of \(C\otimes_A K\). By the induction hypothesis, it follows that, on condition of localizing with respect to a suitable non-zero \(f\) of \(A\), one may assume that \(M'\) is a free \(A\)-module. On the other hand, \(C^m\) is a free \(A\)-module. Hence \(B\) is then a free \(A\)-module, we are finished.

Lemma 8

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(B\) an algebra of finite type over \(A\), \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type, \(\mathfrak{p}\) a prime ideal of \(B\), \(\mathfrak{q}\) the prime ideal it induces on \(A\). One assumes \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) flat over \(A_{\mathfrak{q}}\) (or over \(A\), it is the same). Then there exists a \(g\in B-\mathfrak{p}\) such that

  1. (a)

    \((M/\mathfrak{q}M)_g\) is flat over \(A/\mathfrak{q}\).

  2. (b)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/\mathfrak{q})_g=0\).

Indeed, applying 7 to \((A/\mathfrak{(}q), B/\mathfrak{q}B, M/\mathfrak{q}M)\) one first sees that there exists an \(f\) in \(A-\mathfrak{q}\) such that \((M/\mathfrak{q}M)_f\) is flat over \(A/\mathfrak{q}\). On the other hand, since \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) is flat over \(A\), we have \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/\mathfrak{q})_\mathfrak{p}=\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M_\mathfrak{p},A/\mathfrak{q})=0\), hence, since \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/\mathfrak{q})\) is a \(B\)-module of finite type, there exists a \(g\in B-\mathfrak{p}\) such that (b) holds. One may then (replacing \(g\) by \(gf\)) assume that (a) holds at the same time, which proves the corollary.

Corollary 9

With the notation of 8, for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}'\) of \(B\) containing \(\mathfrak{p}\) and not containing \(g\), \(M_{\mathfrak{p}'}\) is flat over \(A\) (or, what amounts to the same, over \(A_{\mathfrak{q}'}\), where \(\mathfrak{q}'\) is the prime ideal of \(A\) induced by \(\mathfrak{p}')\).

It suffices to apply the criterion 6 (ii) to the system \((A, B_{\mathfrak{q}'}, \mathfrak{q}, M_{\mathfrak{q'}})\), using the localization of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\).

Theorem 10

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and let \(F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\). Let \(U\) be the set of points \(x\in X\) such that \(F_x\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_{f(x)}\). Then \(U\) is an open set.

Proof

One may assume \(X\) and \(Y\) affine, with rings \(B\) and \(A\), so that \(F\) is defined by a \(B\)-module \(M\) of finite type. One applies the criterion 4. Condition (a) is verified trivially by 2 (i), it remains to verify condition (b) of 4. This is what has been done in lemma 8 and corollary 9.

In many questions, the following weaker form of theorem 10 is sufficient (which already follows from lemma 7, and hence requires neither the technique of constructibles, nor theorem 6):

Corollary 11

Under the conditions of 10, if one assumes \(Y\) integral, then there exists a non-empty open \(V\) in \(Y\) such that \(F\) is flat relatively to \(Y\) at all the points of \(f^{-1}(V)\).

Indeed, the open set \(U\) contains the fibre of the generic point of \(Y\) (since the local ring of this point is a field), hence it contains an open of the form \(f^{-1}(V)\), \(X\) being of finite type over \(Y\). From 11, one also concludes easily the following result, where \(Y\) is assumed Noetherian (but not necessarily integral): there exists a partition of \(Y\) into locally closed parts \(Y_i\) such that (endowing \(Y_i\) with the induced reduced structure) \(F\) induces on each \(X_i=X\times_Y Y_i\) a sheaf flat with respect to \(Y_i\).

blocks.html: 6 Flat morphisms and open sets
Let us first recall some results on constructible sets, which are moreover proved in notes in circulation from the Dieudonné-Rosenlicht Seminar on Schemes1.
Let \(X\) be a topological space. Following Chevalley, one says that a subset of \(X\) is constructible if it is a finite union of locally closed subsets.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space, let \(Z\) be a subset of \(X\). For \(Z\) to be constructible, it is necessary and sufficient that for every irreducible closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\), \(Z\cap Y\) be non-dense in \(Y\) or contain a non-empty open subset of the space \(Y\).
From this one deduces, using a well-known lemma of Commutative Algebra:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2 (Chevalley)

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type of preschemes, with \(Y\) Noetherian. Then \(f(X)\) is constructible.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space every irreducible closed subset of which admits a generic point, \(U\) a constructible subset of \(X\), \(x\in X\). For \(U\) to be a neighbourhood of \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that every generization \(y\) of \(x\) (i.e. every \(y\in X\) such that \(x\in \overline{y}\)) lie in \(U\).
In particular

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space every irreducible closed subset of which admits a generic point, \(U\) a subset of \(X\). For \(U\) to be open, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfy the two following conditions:

  1. (a)

    \(U\) contains every generization of each of its points

  2. (b)

    if \(x\in U\), then \(U\cap\overline{x}\) contains a non-empty open subset of the space \(\overline{x}\).
Indeed, \(U\) is necessarily constructible thanks to 1, and one applies the criterion 2 which proves that \(U\) is a neighbourhood of each of its points.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type of preschemes, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(y=f(x)\). For \(f\) to transform every neighbourhood of \(x\) into a neighbourhood of \(y\), it is necessary and sufficient that for every generization \(y'\) of \(y\), there exist a generization \(x'\) of \(x\) such that \(f(x')=y'\).

One may evidently assume that \(X\) and \(Y\) are affine, hence Noetherian. The condition is sufficient, for it suffices to prove that \(f(X)\) is a neighbourhood of \(y\); now \(f(X)\) is constructible by 1, and it suffices to apply the criterion 3. The condition is necessary, for let \(Y'=\overline{y'}\), and let \(F\) be the union of the irreducible components of \(f^{-1}(Y')\) which do not contain \(x\). Then \(X-F\) is an open neighbourhood of \(x\), hence its image is a neighbourhood of \(y\), and a fortiori contains \(y'\), so there exists \(x'_1\in X-F\) such that \(f(x'_1)=y'\). Consider an irreducible component of \(f^{-1}(Y')\) containing \(x'_1\); it necessarily contains \(x\) (for otherwise it would be contained in \(F\)); let \(x'\) be its generic point. It is a generization of \(x\), and \(f(x')\) is a generization of \(f(x'_1)=y'\) contained in \(Y'\), hence is equal to \(y'\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(F\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\) of support \(X\), flat relative to \(Y\). Then \(f\) is an open morphism (i.e., transforms open sets into open sets).

It suffices to prove the criterion 5 for every point \(x\in X\). Now the generizations \(x'\) of \(x\) correspond to the prime ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_x\), those \(y'\) of \(y\) correspond to the prime ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_y\), and one must therefore verify that every prime ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is induced by a prime ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_x\). Now \(F_x\) is a non-zero \(\mathscr{O}_x\)-module and \(\mathscr{O}_y\)-flat, hence faithfully flat over \(\mathscr{O}_y\) by 2. One may therefore apply 3, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks

Since flatness is preserved under base extension, one sees that under the conditions of 5 \(f\) is even universally open. I do not know, however, when \(Y\) is integral and \(X\) of finite type over \(Y\), whether \(f\) induces on every component \(X_i\) of \(X\) an open morphism, or even merely an equidimensional one2, i.e. one all of whose fibre components have the same dimension (one knows only that \(X_i\) dominates \(Y\)). The question is related to the following one: let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism of Noetherian local rings, such that \(B\) is flat over \(A\) and \(\mathfrak{m}B\) is an ideal of definition of \(B\) (which moreover implies \(\dim B=\dim A\)). Is it true that for every minimal prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) of \(B\), one has \(\dim B/\mathfrak{p}_i=\dim B\)? Let us point out only that the answer to the first question is negative when one replaces the flatness hypothesis of 5 by the sole hypothesis that \(f\) be universally open.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7

Let \(A\) be an integral Noetherian ring, \(B\) an \(A\)-algebra of finite type, \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type. Then there exists a non-zero element \(f\) of \(A\) such that \(M_f\) is a free module (a fortiori flat) over \(A_f\).

Let \(K\) be the field of fractions of \(A\); then \(B\otimes_A K\) is an algebra of finite type over \(K\), and \(M\otimes_A K\) a module of finite type over the latter. Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support of this module; we shall argue by induction on \(n\). If \(n<0\), i.e. if \(M\otimes_A K=0\), then taking a finite number of generators of \(M\) over \(B\), one sees that there exists an \(f\in A\) which annihilates these generators, hence \(M\), whence \(M_f=0\) and we have won. Suppose \(n\geqslant 0\). One knows that the \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a composition series whose successive quotients are isomorphic to modules \(B/\mathfrak{p}_i\), the \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) being prime ideals of \(B\). Since an extension of free modules is free, one is reduced to the case where \(M\) itself is of the form \(B/\mathfrak{p}\), or else identical to \(B\), \(B\) being an integral \(A\)-algebra. Applying Noether's normalization lemma to the \(K\)-algebra \(B\otimes_A K\), one sees easily that there exists a non-zero element \(f\) of \(A\) such that \(B_f\) is integral over the subring \(A_f[t_1,\dots,t_n]\), where the \(t_i\) are indeterminates. So one may already assume \(B\) is integral over \(C=A[t_1,\dots,t_n]\); it is therefore a \(C\)-module of finite type without torsion. Let \(m\) be its rank; there exists therefore an exact sequence of \(C\)-modules:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C^m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

where \(M'\) is a torsion \(C\)-module. It follows that the Krull dimension of the \(C\otimes_A K\)-module \(M'\otimes_A K\) is strictly less than that \(n\) of \(C\otimes_A K\). By the induction hypothesis, it follows that, on condition of localizing with respect to a suitable non-zero \(f\) of \(A\), one may assume that \(M'\) is a free \(A\)-module. On the other hand, \(C^m\) is a free \(A\)-module. Hence \(B\) is then a free \(A\)-module, we are finished.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(B\) an algebra of finite type over \(A\), \(M\) a \(B\)-module of finite type, \(\mathfrak{p}\) a prime ideal of \(B\), \(\mathfrak{q}\) the prime ideal it induces on \(A\). One assumes \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) flat over \(A_{\mathfrak{q}}\) (or over \(A\), it is the same). Then there exists a \(g\in B-\mathfrak{p}\) such that

  1. (a)

    \((M/\mathfrak{q}M)_g\) is flat over \(A/\mathfrak{q}\).

  2. (b)

    \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/\mathfrak{q})_g=0\).

Indeed, applying 7 to \((A/\mathfrak{(}q), B/\mathfrak{q}B, M/\mathfrak{q}M)\) one first sees that there exists an \(f\) in \(A-\mathfrak{q}\) such that \((M/\mathfrak{q}M)_f\) is flat over \(A/\mathfrak{q}\). On the other hand, since \(M_\mathfrak{p}\) is flat over \(A\), we have \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/\mathfrak{q})_\mathfrak{p}=\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M_\mathfrak{p},A/\mathfrak{q})=0\), hence, since \(\operatorname{Tor}_1^A(M,A/\mathfrak{q})\) is a \(B\)-module of finite type, there exists a \(g\in B-\mathfrak{p}\) such that (b) holds. One may then (replacing \(g\) by \(gf\)) assume that (a) holds at the same time, which proves the corollary.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

With the notation of 8, for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}'\) of \(B\) containing \(\mathfrak{p}\) and not containing \(g\), \(M_{\mathfrak{p}'}\) is flat over \(A\) (or, what amounts to the same, over \(A_{\mathfrak{q}'}\), where \(\mathfrak{q}'\) is the prime ideal of \(A\) induced by \(\mathfrak{p}')\).
It suffices to apply the criterion 6 (ii) to the system \((A, B_{\mathfrak{q}'}, \mathfrak{q}, M_{\mathfrak{q'}})\), using the localization of the \(\operatorname{Tor}\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 10

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and let \(F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\). Let \(U\) be the set of points \(x\in X\) such that \(F_x\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_{f(x)}\). Then \(U\) is an open set.
Proof

One may assume \(X\) and \(Y\) affine, with rings \(B\) and \(A\), so that \(F\) is defined by a \(B\)-module \(M\) of finite type. One applies the criterion 4. Condition (a) is verified trivially by 2 (i), it remains to verify condition (b) of 4. This is what has been done in lemma 8 and corollary 9.
In many questions, the following weaker form of theorem 10 is sufficient (which already follows from lemma 7, and hence requires neither the technique of constructibles, nor theorem 6):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Under the conditions of 10, if one assumes \(Y\) integral, then there exists a non-empty open \(V\) in \(Y\) such that \(F\) is flat relatively to \(Y\) at all the points of \(f^{-1}(V)\).

Indeed, the open set \(U\) contains the fibre of the generic point of \(Y\) (since the local ring of this point is a field), hence it contains an open of the form \(f^{-1}(V)\), \(X\) being of finite type over \(Y\). From 11, one also concludes easily the following result, where \(Y\) is assumed Noetherian (but not necessarily integral): there exists a partition of \(Y\) into locally closed parts \(Y_i\) such that (endowing \(Y_i\) with the induced reduced structure) \(F\) induces on each \(X_i=X\times_Y Y_i\) a sheaf flat with respect to \(Y_i\).

footnotes.html:
The answer to the second question is
affirmative, that to the first negative even if \(f\)
is étale; cf. EGA IV 12.1.1.5 and EGA Err\(_{\mathrm{I}V}\) 33.
